CHAP. IV. that the administration of the sacrament of baptism by a private person ( one not set apart to the ministry ) be no sacrament , but invalid and to no purpose ; with answer to some objection . first , every sacrament that receive not warrant from the institution of Christ be no sacrament . but baptism by layperson receive no warrant from the institution of Christ , therefore no sacrament . Buc. 46. come . pl of the sacrament . Zanch. of the Sacr of the new Test. 11. this be the hinge of all the rest . the proposition be undeniable , Christ be the instituter and ordainer of every sacrament , to he only it belong to institute a sign of grace ; a sacrament be in the predicament of relation ; and if a man action relate not to this institution , it be but a humane invention , a trick and devise of man . that which give life to a sacrament be divine institution ; if this be want , it be but a piece of forgery . zanch ob . Atho . 1. cap . 24. sacramentum enim ( ut in Ecclesia definiri solet ) est invisibilis gratiae visibile signum ( add ) ex verbo hoc est ex institution christi ad eam remember consecratum , hoc est à prophano usu ad hanc remember sacram translatum ; a sacrament ( as the church have use to define it ) be a visible sign of a invisible grace ( add ) by the word , that be by Christ institution consecrate to that purpose . for , pray you , whence have water virtue to be a sign of such a thing , but from the institution of Christ ? Zanch. in eod . loc . Tom 9 in Ioh. 13. tract . 80. tolle verbum , nullum fuerit sacramentum , take away the word , and it shall be no sacrament . detrahe verbum , ( inquit August : ) and quid erit aqua nisi aqua ? pull away the word , ( say S. § . that this one great sacred and solemn covenant of our covenant in baptism once for all . ( in which the blood and body of Christ slay for we , be give to we , and take by we , as token of our troth and fidelity , no less than of God ) do bind we , as man and Christians to all duty we owe to God and man , self and other , private and public in ecclesiastical or civil society and policy ; by this we stand oblige to fight against the world , the flesh and the devil , to deny all ungodliness and worldly lust , to live righteous , sober , and godly in this present world , that be , conformable to God law and Christ command ; also to those good law and constitution of justice , order , government and peace , which be establish in any kingdom or state , though not Christian ; how much more , if Christian and in a national church-communion ; this be affirm and confirm by dr. Gauden . § . also that to renew this baptismal vow and Evangelical covenant by daily repentance and frequent celebration of the lord supper , be very commendable as to private and public piety . § . but to add to this any public solemn covenant , under a new form of word in a national notion , where be no national apostasy from true Christianity , nor yet any Anabaptistical renounce of our baptism , and to do this without due authority , make a part of the body to covenant for the whole , and this without the head , and in opposition to law yet in force , and rule of civil justice , as to many man lawful estate , deserve honour , and due authority ; this kind of covenant dr. James Douglas , a Scotchman , etc courteous reader , SCotland be the seat of war , on which all Europe ( at this present ) have fix a most steadfast and vigilent eye ; and consider that there be many thing of great concernment and pertinency , that have not as yet be present to public view : i shall therefore endeavour to represent thou with a most faithful and infallible Narrative in relation thereunto , and the first thing that i insist upon shall be , the strange and wonderful prophesy of a dumb scot , live in the town of sterling , age about 50. and write with his own hand , which follow in these word , viz. the war shall begîn in the spring , much woe to Scotland it shall bring ; then shall the lad cry well away , that ever we live to see this day . for the begin of the month of may , 165 1. a great battle shall be fight between the english and the Scot , and that for the space of 10 hour , the victory shall seem both dublous and doubtful ; but at last it shall fall to the english , and their head shall be crown with the trophy thereof ; insomuch , that above 20000 Scots shall be total subdue , their Lieut. Gen. and many other officer of note , take and kill ; and their king himself enforce to a poor cottage in the north island with a small party , where shall come a old woman with a axe , and shall say to he , what be thou ? and he shall answer and say , with tear trick•ing upon his cheek , i be the king of scots : then shall she make this reply , make haste and be go for thy enemy be near at hand , and betake thy self to the island of Orkney , where a strong band of man shall unite together for thy safeguard and security : this be do , he shall they attempt to take the field again , which shall prove more fatal than the former ; insomuch , that treachery , either by sword or bullet , threaten to take he off , and above 20. nobleman shall loose their head . then shall the english summon sterling castle ; and towards the latter end of June , it shall be surrender : after this saint johnstons shall yield , and many other formidable castle in the north ; but the quarrel disputable till August , at which time a agreement shall be make , and the whole confine of Scotland , become subject and obedient to the Parl. of England ; the scotch king shall end his day in the field , and none of that race or family for the future , shall annoy either England , Scotland , or Ireland . and further , this dumb prophet verify at what time this great battle shall be fight , which verse with great art , he enclose in a paper , and send to the king , a copy whereof followetn . the 15 of this month of May , shall be a sad disastrous day ; and they that Charles his part do take shall dear suffer for his sake : and many of his friend shall fly , like dust before the enemy these line be write by the author , and his name with this forth reposition prefix . sign by James Douglas , a enemy to the usurp norman race , a cordial friend to truth and unspotted liberty , and a loyal native to the freedom of his country , etc the young king have no soon peruse these despicable line , but he wax exceed wroth , give strict charge and command , that he shall forthwith be commit to safe custody ; and accord he be with great rigour send to sterling castle , where he now remain close prisoner ; but by the help of some friend , promise pen , ink , and paper , write this ensue declaration . God the absolute sovereign lord and king of all thing in heaven and earth , the original fountain and cause of all cause , who be circumscribe , govern , and limit by no rule , but do all thing mere and only by his sovereign will and unlimited good pleasure , who make the world , and all thing therein , for his own glory , and who by his own will and pleasure give man ( his mere creature ) the sovereignty ( under himself ) over all the rest of his creature , and endue he with a rational soul or understanding , and thereby create he after his own image , and by his sovereign and absolute create power , make a female or woman , call Eve , which two , and the earthly original fountain , as begetter , and bringer forth of all and every particular and individual man , and woman , that ever breathe in the world sink , who be and be by nature all alike in power , dignity , authority , and majesty , none of they have any authority , dominion , or magisterial power , one over or above another , but by instituon or donation , that be to say , by mutual agreement or consent give , derive or assume by mutual consent and agreement , for the good , benefit , and comfort each of other , and not for the mischief , hurt , or damage of any , it be unnatural , irrational , sinful , wicked , and unjust for any man or man whatsoever , to part with so much of their power , as shall inable a prince to destroy and undo they therewith . and unnatural , irrational , sinful , wicked , unjust , devilish , and tyrannical ; it be for a prince to appropriate and assume unto himself , a power , authority and jurisdiction to unite , govern , or reign over any sort of man in the world , without their free consent ; and he that do it , do thereby as much as in he lie , endeavour to appropriate and assume unto himself the office and sovereignty of God , who alone do , and be to rule by his will and pleasure . and wickedness [ in the high ] it be for any king to reign and govern by his prerogative , will and pleasure ; although his late father Charles the first , will needs maintain this erroneous maxim , that kingdom be king own , and that they may do with they what they will ; as if kingdom be for they , and not they for their kingdom : therefore again , i say , that their way be wicked , unjust , and tyrannical ; and as it be a great wickedness for any sort of man o•suffer they so to do , so it be a great sin and presumption of all god saint , if they do not use all possible mean for the cast off the yoke of usurpation and tyranny . from my captivity in sterling castle , this 8. of Jan. 1651. CHAP. ix . of the affection and passion of the soul Chap. 9 book 1. it remain to treat of the affection and passion which be incident to the soul , and be but as it be the sundry fashion and form of the appetitive soul , which Aristotle sometime call accident , sometime affection , sometime passion of the soul affection as they be moderate , natural , and engender with the soul and body , be action transient , not permanent , and so be neither vicious or evil in themselves , nor ought nor can be remove from our nature , which be the error of some philosopher of the sect of the stoic ; passion as they be inordinate and predominant , and so be vicious , and dangerous to the soul , and both may and aught to be expel , such as fall not on a philosopher , that be to say , a wise and virtuous man , for virtus consistit in moderation passionum ; therefore although to be angry be natural to every man , and can be expel the soul of any , yet to be inordinate and immoderate in anger be the part of a fool , and not of a wise man , because where any inordinate passion do bear rule , they cloud the understanding , and obscure the light of truth , as thick cloud the light of the sun ; and therefore conclude Aristotle in the four of his ethic , Mansuetus vult imperturbatus esse , and non duci à passion , from a wise man all unruly and disorder passion be to be drive away . this be the exhortation give by philosophy it self to Boetius — — tu quo { que } si vis lumine claro cernere verum , Tramite recto carpere coelum , Gaudia pelle , pelle timorem , spem { que } fugato , nec dolour assit , nubila mens est vincta { que } fraenis haec ubi regnant , — and though there be several other affection and passion of the soul which be not here nominate , but be notwithstanding in themselves as vicious as these , yet these of joy , fear , hope , and grief , be the four cardinal and chief affection unto which all the rest may be reduce ; for there be no affection but be in respect of some good or evil , and that a good or evil present , or good and evil absent : joy be a affection of the soul for a good present ; hope a affection of the soul for a good absent and future ; grief be for a evil present ; fear for a evil absent and future ; which affection as long as they be but right , ordinate , and subject , nor may , nor can be expel be natural and good , but when they grow heady , sensual , fleshy , and terrene , set upon the lust and pleasure of this stageplay world , be vicious , and hurtful , which a wise man and a virtuous will keep under , and not suffer to range and rule ; for these be they which be proper call passion , when they grow exorbitant , accord to the definition of Aquinas , affectus est vehemens animi passio animum torquens and verum judicium rationis impediens ; otherwise it be of these natural and ordinate affection , for they do neither animum torquere , nor verum rationis judicium impedire , so be not evil in themselves , and of their own nature , but through error in man judgement , from whence the vitiosity pass upon the affection : other reason may be give of the evil and exorbitant passion which doubtless be strong or weak according to the temperature of the four element in the body of man , from whence the complexion have their denomination ; if the complexion be sanguine , it common feed the affection of joy , and mirth , and love , and the like ; if choleric , expect anger , hatred , malice , etc if melancholy , than sorrow , fear , and grief ; and thus accord to the temperature of the body be passion for the most part more or less predominant ; the more temperate the complexion , the more moderate the passion , the better the constitution , the pure and noble than affection be . that all affection of the soul be vicious , and not only to be moderate , but whole to be extirpate and expel from our nature , be a error broach in the school of the stoic , condemn in Christianity , as well as by the peripatetic philosopher of old . mr. Hooker . it be not in our own power whether we will be stir with affection or no ; it be as possible to prevent they all as to go out of our self , or to give our self a new nature ; no more than we can refuse to wink with the eye when a sudden blow be offer at it , or refuse to yawn when we see a yawn sleepy fellow , though by frequent and habitual mortification , and by continual watchfulness , dr. tailor life of Christ 2 part folio 122. and stand in readiness against all in advertency we may lessen the inclination , and account few sudden irreption , say a devout and judicious doctor of our english church . many aught to be correct , few total to be reject ; some affection there be which be virtuous and godly in themselves ; some be wicked and diabolical , some be in themselves neither godly nor wicked . the good and virtuous be , love , pity , joy , charity , etc the diabolical be , envy , wrath , malice , and especial that which your Greeks call nonlatinalphabet , that be , a rejoice at other man ill hap and misfortune ; the indifferent affection be , fear , sorrow , anger , and the like , which as they be not simple good , so be they not moral evil , since our bless saviour , who know no sin , be notwithstanding subject to like passion of sorrow , fear , grief , and the like ; such as accrue to we by the fall of our first parent , and be infirmity and defect of pure nature , effect or fruit of sin , and so a evil , to wit of punishment , as well as other bodily defect , as hunger , thirst , sickness , yea and death , which come unto we but by the fall , all these our saviour be subject to , in all thing be make like unto we , sin only except . the evil of sin he take not , though he take upon he the true nature of man , so say saint Augustine , as Christ take upon he a true humane nature , so he take the true defect and evil of that nature , but not all ; he take upon he the defect or evil of punishment , but not the defect and evil of sin : such passion and infirmity of fear , grief , anger , of sickness , death , and the like , as be evil of punishment only , Christ be subject to as we be , therefore such be not simple sinful , neither can they be simple good , since they be effect and fruit of sin . nor be Christ subject to these passion in the same manner and measure as we , Christ only in the first motion , and sudden irresistible alteration , those twinckling of the eye as the philosopher call they , those propassion as the schoolman term they , or passion transient ; but we not in propassion only for a flash and away , but in passion also , in passion permanent to entertain it , and retain it , many time either without just cause , or long than occasion require , or beyond due measure , by which the affection come to be inordinate , the mind move , perplex and trouble , reason blind , judgement pervert and deprave . these be the disease and malady of my soul , far worse than any that can be of my body , whether it be lethargy , frenzy , apoplexy , epilepsy , burn fever , or the like , all which be the most dangerous disease of the body ; for though the outward sense may be surprise by these , and my body thereby make insensible of pain , yet while my soul remain un-distempered , my reason be able to discover and judge of these bodily distemper , either by its inflammation , or beat of its pulse and artery , or by some extraordinary heat and lassitude ; but in the way of more strict religion it be advise that he that will cure his passion shall pray often ; dr. tailor life of Christ par . 2. folio 125. a remedy against passion in general . it be saint augustines counsel unto the bishop Auxilius , that like the apostle in a storm , we shall awaken Christ , and call to he for aid , lest we shipwreck in so violent passion , and impetuous disturbance ; upon which the rest be found , from whence they spring , ( viz. ) love and ire . anger though simple , and as it be in it self consider , to wit , in its first motion and natural inclination , be neither good nor evil , yet be make good or evil accord to the circumstance of time , and adjunct of manner , and measure ;
